guides.title: Fire Making
guides.summary: Prepare a safe fire site, build a small structure of tinder, kindling, and fuel, then light the tinder and add wood gradually.
guides.quickAnswer: Prepare a safe fire site, build a small structure of tinder, kindling, and fuel, then light the tinder and add wood gradually.
whenToUse: Outdoor situations where a fire is allowed and safe, for warmth, signaling, or boiling water.
guides.preferredAction: Use dry fine tinder, small kindling, and then larger fuel, protecting the flame from wind and keeping the fire small.
guides.backupAction: If matches or a lighter are weak, use multiple small tinder bundles and shield them carefully while lighting.
stepByStepActions: Confirm local rules and safety before building a fire.
Clear the fire area down to mineral soil and away from overhanging branches, tents, and flammables.
Gather three fuel sizes: tinder, kindling, and larger fuel wood.
Arrange tinder in a small bundle and build kindling around it with space for air.
Light the tinder from the windward side and add more kindling, then larger wood, as the fire strengthens.
warnings: Even small fires can spread quickly in dry or windy conditions; embers can ignite nearby vegetation.
whatNotToDo: Do not use gasoline or unknown flammable liquids.
Do not leave a fire unattended or build it against roots, logs, or peat soils.
redFlags: Flames spreading beyond your cleared area or wind pushing sparks toward structures or vegetation.
preparednessTips: Carry waterproof matches or a lighter and reliable tinder in a waterproof container when traveling outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-building guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common safety-focused outdoor fire-starting practice, adapted to local rules.

guides.title: Knot Tying
guides.parentTopic: Cordage, Knots, and Safe Tool Use
guides.summary: Use adjustable knots that can tension and re-tension guy lines to keep tarps and bivouacs stable.
guides.quickAnswer: Use adjustable knots that can tension and re-tension guy lines to keep tarps and bivouacs stable.
whenToUse: Any time you need to tension lines for tarps, tents, or improvised shelters.
guides.preferredAction: Use a taut-line hitch on guy lines to create an adjustable loop that slides when slack and grips when loaded.
guides.backupAction: Use simple wraps around trees and multiple half hitches if you do not know the named knots, understanding they are less adjustable.
stepByStepActions: Wrap cord around a stake or tree and create an adjustable loop in the standing line.
For a taut-line hitch, make two wraps inside the loop toward the anchor and one wrap outside, then tighten.
Slide the knot to adjust tension on the tarp as needed.
For a trucker’s hitch, create a loop in the standing line, run the working end through an anchor point and back through the loop, then pull to tension and secure with half hitches.
warnings: Incorrectly tied knots can slip and cause shelter failure in bad weather.
High loads on thin cord can cut into bark or damage anchors.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload light cordage with heavy loads such as body weight.
Do not rely on camping knots for technical rescue or climbing.
redFlags: Shelter repeatedly collapsing in severe weather when you cannot maintain warmth and dryness.
preparednessTips: Practice a very small set of knots at home until you can tie them under stress.
Carry several meters of strong cord or paracord in your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Knots for Survival and Rescue

guides.title: Shelter Building
guides.summary: Get off wet ground, reduce wind exposure, and create simple overhead cover with whatever materials you have.
guides.quickAnswer: Get off wet ground, reduce wind exposure, and create simple overhead cover with whatever materials you have.
whenToUse: When stranded outdoors for many hours or overnight without a tent, especially in cold, wet, or windy conditions.
guides.preferredAction: Use a tarp or plastic sheet with cord to make a low, wind-sheltered lean-to or A-frame near a natural windbreak.
guides.backupAction: If you have no sheet or tarp, use branches, leaves, and other natural materials to build a simple debris shelter that blocks wind and insulates you from the ground.
stepByStepActions: Choose a spot above drainage channels and away from falling branches or rockfall.
Use trees, rocks, or trekking poles as supports for a tarp or sheet; pitch it low and tight with the open side away from wind.
Build a thick insulating layer under you using dry leaves, pine boughs, packs, or spare clothing.
Keep the shelter small so body heat can warm the space more effectively.
Keep your core and head insulated and change out of wet clothes if you have dry layers.
warnings: Improvised shelters offer limited protection; hypothermia is still possible in wet, windy, or very cold conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not build in dry riverbeds, on ridge tops in storms, or under dead branches (“widow-makers”).
redFlags: Numb hands or feet progressing to clumsiness or confusion.
Inability to stop shivering after shelter and dry layers.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one emergency tarp or space blanket, cord, and a foam pad in outdoor kits.

guides.title: Land Navigation
guides.summary: Use map and compass if you have them; otherwise use only broad terrain awareness for cautious short decisions, and avoid ambitious travel when lost.
guides.quickAnswer: Use map and compass if you have them; otherwise use only broad terrain awareness for cautious short decisions, and avoid ambitious travel when lost.
whenToUse: Use when GPS has failed, battery is low, or accuracy is poor and you need a fallback strategy on land.
guides.preferredAction: Use map and compass together to orient yourself and plan short safe movement along obvious terrain handrails like roads, trails, ridges, or major valleys.
guides.backupAction: If you lack tools or skill, use the sun and major terrain only for broad orientation, not precise navigation, and prefer staying put over long uncertain travel.
stepByStepActions: If you have a map and compass, orient the map to north and identify obvious terrain features around you.
Match roads, rivers, ridges, valleys, and lakes to the map.
Plan short, conservative travel legs along clear handrails.
If visibility is poor or your location estimate is unstable, stop traveling and shelter instead.
If you have no tools, use the sun only for general direction and avoid relying on folk methods as precise navigation.
warnings: Many natural-navigation folklore tricks are unreliable.
Fog, storms, darkness, and whiteout conditions can defeat even experienced navigators.
Streams and valleys do not automatically lead safely to help.
whatNotToDo: Do not over-trust smartphone navigation if battery is low or the device is cold.
Do not commit to long dead-reckoning travel in unfamiliar terrain.
Do not treat moss, plant growth, or similar folklore as reliable directional tools.
redFlags: You repeatedly cannot match terrain to your map or keep changing your estimated position.
Visibility drops to the point that safe travel is no longer realistic.
You are becoming exhausted, hypothermic, or heat-stressed while trying to navigate.
preparednessTips: Carry a paper map and simple baseplate compass even if using GPS.
Practice map and compass skills in easy terrain before relying on them remotely.
Learn local terrain features and hazards before your trip.

Know how to turn off utilities if trained and safe to do so.

guides.title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help
guides.summary: Call emergency services immediately, move the person to a cooler place, and start rapid cooling; do not give fluids to a confused or unconscious person.
guides.quickAnswer: Call emergency services immediately, move the person to a cooler place, and start rapid cooling; do not give fluids to a confused or unconscious person.

guides.preferredAction: Activate emergency medical services at once and start aggressive cooling with cool water, wet sheets, and airflow.

stepByStepActions: Call emergency services as soon as heat stroke is suspected.
Move the person to the coolest accessible place and remove excess clothing.
Cool the body rapidly with cool water, wet cloths or sheets, or a cool bath if safe.
Fan the person while keeping the skin wet to increase evaporation.
If vomiting or unconscious, position on the side if safe and maintain the airway.

warnings: Heat stroke can cause permanent organ damage or death.

whatNotToDo: Do not give fluids if the person is confused, vomiting, or unconscious.
Do not delay calling for help while trying home measures first.
Do not use alcohol rubs or extreme direct ice exposure over large areas as a substitute for standard cooling.

guides.title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help

guides.summary: If the person cannot cough, speak, or breathe, give back blows and abdominal thrusts (or chest thrusts when appropriate) and call for help.

Lean them forward and give up to 5 firm back blows.

warnings: Use appropriate force and technique for size and age.

whatNotToDo: Do not do blind finger sweeps in a responsive person.
Do not interrupt an effective cough with severe choking maneuvers.
redFlags: Any severe choking episode requires emergency help; persistent breathing difficulty after the object is removed also needs review.
preparednessTips: Encourage formal first-aid and CPR training.

guides.title: Lost on Land: First 30 Minutes
guides.summary: Stop, calm down, assess your situation, try only a short safe backtrack if the route is obvious, then stay put, shelter, and start signaling.
guides.quickAnswer: Stop, calm down, assess your situation, try only a short safe backtrack if the route is obvious, then stay put, shelter, and start signaling.

Control breathing and avoid panic.
Check time, weather, terrain, last known point, and available gear.
If a short, obvious retrace is safe, try it once slowly.

Call or text for help if possible and share landmarks or GPS.
Pick a safer base area away from immediate hazards.
Start signaling with whistle, bright items, and light if needed.
Conserve warmth, water, and battery.

Poor visibility and worsening weather sharply increase travel risk.
Night movement without a clear route is high-risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep wandering without a deliberate plan.
Do not split the group.
Do not waste phone battery on non-essential use.

Weather is worsening and you lack good shelter.
You are in avalanche terrain, flood drainages, cliff areas, or fire zones and need to move only far enough to reach safer ground.

guides.title: Stay Put vs Move (Land)
guides.summary: If you are truly lost but not in immediate danger, staying put is usually safer and makes you easier to find; move only to escape danger or reach clearly safer ground.
guides.quickAnswer: If you are truly lost but not in immediate danger, staying put is usually safer and makes you easier to find; move only to escape danger or reach clearly safer ground.
whenToUse: When you realize you are off-route or stranded and are deciding whether to stay or try to self-rescue.
guides.preferredAction: Stay put near your last known location, improve shelter, and make yourself visible unless there is an immediate life threat where you are.
guides.backupAction: If staying is clearly unsafe, move carefully to the nearest safer, more visible, and more sheltered area, then stop and re-establish your plan.
stepByStepActions: Identify immediate dangers such as rising water, unstable slopes, falling rocks, fire, lightning exposure, or traffic.
If no immediate danger exists, stay near your last known location where rescuers will likely search first.
Improve shelter and visibility in a spot that is safer but still findable.
If you must move, choose a conservative destination such as slightly higher ground above flood level or a safer forest edge.
Move slowly and avoid steep drop-offs, fast water crossings, avalanche paths, and dense terrain.
Once in a safer spot, stop again, mark your location if possible, and begin signaling.
warnings: Wandering far from your last known route can drastically enlarge search areas and delay rescue.
Self-rescue over difficult terrain without skills or equipment leads to many injuries and fatalities.
whatNotToDo: Do not descend blindly into gullies, canyons, or rivers just because downhill might lead to people.
Do not cross fast-moving water above your knees without training and proper gear.
Do not split your group unless absolutely necessary.
redFlags: Very poor shelter options with severe weather imminent.
Active fire, unstable ground, or advancing water in your area.
A severely injured person who cannot be moved safely from a dangerous location.
preparednessTips: Learn local terrain hazards before trips.
Carry a paper map and compass and know basic use.
Tell someone your route and turnaround time so search can begin from the right place.

guides.title: Whistle Distress Signals (Land)
guides.summary: Use three short whistle blasts repeated regularly as your basic distress signal.
guides.quickAnswer: Use three short whistle blasts repeated regularly as your basic distress signal.
whenToUse: Any time you are lost, injured, or need help and think searchers or other people may be within hearing range.
guides.preferredAction: Blow three short blasts on a whistle, wait 30 to 60 seconds, and repeat.
guides.backupAction: If you have no whistle, shout in patterns, bang metal objects, or use a radio or phone if available.
stepByStepActions: Carry the whistle on your person so it is always reachable.
Give three strong blasts, each about one second long.
Pause for 30 to 60 seconds to listen and conserve energy, then repeat.
If you hear two blasts or a vocal response, continue giving short blasts every 10 to 15 seconds to guide rescuers in.
If using a radio or phone, keep whistle signaling intermittent and consistent.
warnings: Continuous blowing or yelling can exhaust you and make direction-finding harder for rescuers.
A whistle is less effective in strong wind, near loud rivers, or with helicopter noise.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on your voice.
Do not give random patterns; consistent three-blast signals are easier to recognize as distress.
redFlags: Use whistle signals immediately if anyone is injured, unable to move, or stuck in dangerous terrain.
If no response comes and conditions are worsening, shift more effort toward shelter and self-protection while continuing intermittent signals.
preparednessTips: Carry a pea-less plastic whistle on all trips, attached to your person not just your pack.

Reserve the safest water for drinking, infant formula, food preparation, brushing teeth, and wound cleaning.

guides.title: Fast Tarp Lean-To for Wind and Rain

guides.title: Keeping Children, Elderly, and Frail Adults Warm Safely
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guides.summary: Keep vulnerable people in the warmest room, use safe layers and blankets, check them often, and avoid unsafe heating methods.
guides.quickAnswer: Keep vulnerable people in the warmest room, use safe layers and blankets, check them often, and avoid unsafe heating methods.
whenToUse: Cold homes, power outages, or outdoor delays involving infants, older adults, or people with illness or disability.
guides.preferredAction: Move vulnerable people to the warmest sealed room, keep them off the floor, and monitor for early signs of hypothermia.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot keep them safely warm, relocate them to a friend’s home, healthcare facility, or official shelter if possible.
stepByStepActions: Move vulnerable people to the warmest, best-sealed room and keep them off the floor.
Dress them in multiple layers and cover head, hands, and feet.
Offer warm drinks if they are awake and can swallow safely.
Check for unusual fatigue, confusion, slurred speech, or very cold skin.
Keep heaters and open flames away from bedding and clothing.
warnings: Children, older adults, and people with chronic illness can develop hypothermia faster and may not report symptoms clearly.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on hot water bottles, electric blankets, or heaters directly against skin for people who cannot move away or feel burns.
Do not use unsafe fuel-burning heaters indoors.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, extreme sleepiness, or bluish/pale very cold skin.
preparednessTips: Plan in advance where vulnerable household members could stay if your home becomes too cold.
Ensure smoke and CO alarms work and have backup power.

guides.title: Keeping Dry in Rain and Snow Outdoors
guides.summary: Use water-resistant outer layers, protect hands and feet, and minimize time in heavy rain or slush to prevent dangerous chilling.
guides.quickAnswer: Use water-resistant outer layers, protect hands and feet, and minimize time in heavy rain or slush to prevent dangerous chilling.
whenToUse: Outdoor travel or work in wet, cold weather, especially when returning to shelter may be delayed.
guides.preferredAction: Wear waterproof or water-resistant outer layers, keep spare dry socks and gloves, and avoid soaking your clothing when possible.
guides.backupAction: If you lack waterproof gear, improvise with ponchos or plastic over critical areas and reduce exposure time.
stepByStepActions: Put on or improvise a water-resistant shell over insulating layers.
Protect shoes and socks and change into dry socks when you can.
Keep gloves or mittens as dry as possible and carry a spare pair if available.
Limit time standing in slush, puddles, or wet snow.
Once under shelter, remove wet outer layers and hang them to dry.
warnings: Wet clothing greatly increases heat loss and hypothermia risk in cold or windy weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking in wet cotton layers if you can change.
Do not ignore wet feet and hands.
redFlags: Shivering that does not stop after shelter and dry clothes, or progressing clumsiness or confusion.
preparednessTips: Carry simple rain protection and at least one spare pair of socks during cold seasons.

guides.title: Drying Wet Clothing and Bedding Safely Indoors
guides.summary: Hang wet items spread out in the warmest, best-ventilated space you have, away from heaters or flames that could ignite them.
guides.quickAnswer: Hang wet items spread out in the warmest, best-ventilated space you have, away from heaters or flames that could ignite them.
whenToUse: After getting wet during cold weather once you are in an indoor or sheltered area.
guides.preferredAction: Use clotheslines, hangers, or chairs to air-dry items while keeping them at least three feet from heaters.
guides.backupAction: If heat is limited, prioritize drying socks, gloves, and base layers; wring items thoroughly and rotate them closer to safe heat sources while supervising constantly.
stepByStepActions: Wring out wet clothes and bedding away from sleeping areas.
Hang items so air can circulate; avoid piling wet items in heaps.
Keep all fabrics at least three feet away from heaters, stoves, or open flames.
Ventilate briefly if possible to reduce dampness and mold risk.
Put on dry layers while items dry rather than waiting in wet clothing.
warnings: Clothing or bedding too close to heaters is a common cause of home fires.
whatNotToDo: Do not drape clothes directly on heaters or over open-flame devices.
Do not leave heaters unattended while drying items.
redFlags: Smoke, scorch marks, burning smell, or any small fire that is not immediately controllable.
preparednessTips: Keep clips or rope for indoor drying lines and maintain working smoke alarms near heating areas.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses keeping combustibles three feet from heat sources.

guides.title: Managing Moisture Inside Tents and Improvised Shelters
guides.summary: Ventilate enough to reduce condensation while keeping wind and rain out, and keep sleeping areas off wet ground.
guides.quickAnswer: Ventilate enough to reduce condensation while keeping wind and rain out, and keep sleeping areas off wet ground.
whenToUse: When sleeping in tents or tarp shelters in damp, cold conditions.
guides.preferredAction: Use ground insulation, open small vents or gaps, and keep wet gear separate from sleeping areas.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot ventilate well, wipe condensation periodically and rotate damp items out of sleeping areas.
stepByStepActions: Pitch on slightly higher ground to avoid pooling water.
Use a groundsheet or tarp under the sleeping area, slightly smaller than the tent floor.
Open small vents or gaps at the top or downwind side to let moisture escape.
Keep wet clothing and boots away from sleeping insulation.
Wipe interior condensation with a cloth if it builds up.
warnings: Humidity and condensation can chill you overnight and dampen insulation, increasing hypothermia risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not fully seal all vents while cooking or using fuel appliances in or near the shelter.
redFlags: Drowsiness, headaches, or nausea while any fuel-burning device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Carry a small groundsheet and cloth for wiping condensation; learn your tent vents before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Weather Camping and Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Discusses keeping tents dry and managing moisture.

guides.title: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet
guides.summary: Get under cover, remove wet layers if possible, insulate yourself from the ground, and warm your core with dry clothes or shared insulation.
guides.quickAnswer: Get under cover, remove wet layers if possible, insulate yourself from the ground, and warm your core with dry clothes or shared insulation.
whenToUse: Unexpected overnight in cold, wet conditions where you or others are already soaked.
guides.preferredAction: Use any available shelter, change into dry layers, and use dry blankets or sleeping bags to protect the core first.
guides.backupAction: If no dry clothes exist, wring out wet layers, put them back on, and maximize insulation with natural materials and shared coverings.
stepByStepActions: Move immediately to the best available shelter.
Remove wet upper-body clothing and replace it with anything dry; protect the head and torso well.
Lay down thick insulation under you using packs, branches, leaves, cardboard, or extra clothing.
Wrap in all available blankets, sleeping bags, or dry fabrics.
Eat and drink if safe to fuel heat production and avoid more exposure outside the shelter.
warnings: Remaining in wet clothing in cold conditions can lead to hypothermia even above freezing temperatures.
whatNotToDo: Do not sit or sleep directly on bare ground or metal.
Do not rely only on a fire if building one would be slow, unsafe, or impossible.
redFlags: Any signs of moderate hypothermia such as confusion, slurred speech, or stumbling despite warming efforts.
preparednessTips: Pack at least one fully dry emergency set of base layers and socks in a waterproof bag for cold/wet trips.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Advises staying dry and warm.

guides.title: Overnight Survival in a Vehicle in Cold Weather
guides.summary: Stay in the vehicle, insulate yourself, run the engine only periodically with a clear exhaust pipe, and avoid sleeping with continuous engine or heater use.
guides.quickAnswer: Stay in the vehicle, insulate yourself, run the engine only periodically with a clear exhaust pipe, and avoid sleeping with continuous engine or heater use.
whenToUse: When stranded in a vehicle during winter storms or extreme cold.
guides.preferredAction: Remain in the vehicle, keep visible to rescuers, and manage warmth with clothing, blankets, and carefully limited engine use.
guides.backupAction: If staying is unsafe, such as due to buried exhaust, fuel leak, or another immediate hazard, move to a safer nearby shelter only if you can do so without getting lost or badly exposed.
stepByStepActions: Stay with your vehicle; it is easier to find than a person on foot.
Check that the exhaust pipe is not blocked by snow or debris before running the engine.
Run the engine and heater only long enough to warm the interior, then shut it off to conserve fuel and reduce CO risk.
Open a window slightly on the side away from the wind for ventilation.
Wear all available layers and use blankets, sleeping bags, or extra clothing for insulation.
warnings: Blocked exhaust pipes or running engines in enclosed spaces can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not sleep with the engine running continuously.
Do not use charcoal grills or camp stoves inside the vehicle.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion while the engine is running.
Snow building up around the vehicle.
preparednessTips: Keep a winter car kit with blankets, hats, gloves, shovel, traction aid, food, water, and flashlight.
Maintain at least a half tank of fuel in winter.

guides.title: Safe Fire Site Selection and Wind Management
guides.summary: Choose an open, clear area away from vegetation, roots, and overhead branches, and account for wind so sparks and smoke blow away from people and shelter.
guides.quickAnswer: Choose an open, clear area away from vegetation, roots, and overhead branches, and account for wind so sparks and smoke blow away from people and shelter.
whenToUse: Any time you plan to make an outdoor fire.
guides.preferredAction: Use existing fire rings or designated sites whenever possible; otherwise clear down to mineral soil and keep a bare radius around the fire.
guides.backupAction: If the ground cannot be cleared safely, skip the fire and rely on shelter, clothing, and hot drinks instead.
stepByStepActions: Look up and around; avoid branches, tents, tarps, and dry grass.
Select bare soil, sand, or gravel if possible and clear a 1–2 meter radius of flammable material.
Note wind direction and keep sparks blowing into nonflammable areas.
Keep water, dirt, or sand nearby to extinguish the fire quickly.
Keep the fire small and manageable.
warnings: Wildfire risk can be extreme in dry or windy conditions; authorities may restrict all open flame.
whatNotToDo: Do not build fires in high winds, during fire bans, inside or under shelters, or directly on peat or organic soils.
redFlags: Rapidly moving grass fire, uncontrolled flames, or smoke threatening people or structures.
preparednessTips: Check local fire danger ratings and learn at least one warming method that does not rely on open flame.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-safety principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard fire-agency messaging on site selection, spark control, and fire bans.

guides.title: Choosing and Preparing Dry Fuel
guides.summary: Collect small, dead, and dry wood from off the ground and sheltered areas, and split larger pieces to expose drier inner wood if you have the skill and tools.
guides.quickAnswer: Collect small, dead, and dry wood from off the ground and sheltered areas, and split larger pieces to expose drier inner wood if you have the skill and tools.
whenToUse: Any outdoor fire building, especially after rain or snow.
guides.preferredAction: Gather a generous supply of small, dry fuel before lighting; prioritize dead branches from standing trees and sheltered spots.
guides.backupAction: If most wood is damp, split thicker pieces to access drier cores and use extra tinder and kindling.
stepByStepActions: Look for dead branches still on trees or under logs and rocks where they stayed drier.
Snap small twigs; if they break crisply, they are more likely to be dry enough.
Collect plenty of kindling and medium sticks before lighting.
If experienced and properly equipped, split thicker pieces to expose dry inner surfaces.
Keep your driest fuel under cover once collected.
warnings: Handling cutting tools when cold and tired increases injury risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not cut live trees or branches in protected areas.
Do not rely on a tiny fuel pile and assume you can gather more after dark.
redFlags: You cannot find or prepare enough dry fuel to stay warm and are already cold and wet.
preparednessTips: Carry a small amount of known-dry fuel or prepared tinder where wood may be scarce or wet.

sourceReferences.title: Fuel-selection strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely taught fuel-selection and preparation techniques for outdoor fires.

guides.title: Finding and Using Dry Tinder in Wet Weather
guides.summary: Look in sheltered places for the driest fine material, and use more tinder than usual to overcome dampness.
guides.quickAnswer: Look in sheltered places for the driest fine material, and use more tinder than usual to overcome dampness.
whenToUse: Rain, snow, or soaked ground make normal fire starting difficult, but a fire is still safe and allowed.
guides.preferredAction: Use carried tinder plus the driest inner wood or bark you can process into fine shavings.
guides.backupAction: If you have no carried tinder, collect dry inner wood, dead standing twigs, and fibrous bark and process them into thin curls or fibers.
stepByStepActions: Check your kit first for dry tinder stored in waterproof packaging.
Look under logs, under overhangs, or inside dead standing wood for dry material.
Shave or scrape dry inner wood or bark into thin curls or fibers.
Build a large tinder bundle, then surround it with very fine kindling.
Shield your tinder from rain with your body, pack, or tarp while lighting.
warnings: Fire starting in wet conditions is skill-intensive and may fail; do not rely on it as your only survival plan.
whatNotToDo: Do not strip bark from live trees in protected areas.
Do not waste your only ignition source on poorly prepared tinder.
redFlags: Repeated failed attempts while you are becoming colder and wetter.
preparednessTips: Always carry some dry tinder and a waterproof ignition source; do not assume you can improvise in all conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Field-oriented hypothermia prevention guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing dryness and shelter and acknowledges the difficulty of wet-weather fire starting.

guides.title: Building a Fire Lay That Survives Rain and Snow
guides.summary: Raise the fire off soaked ground, protect it from above, and stack fuel so it can dry and catch as the fire grows.
guides.quickAnswer: Raise the fire off soaked ground, protect it from above, and stack fuel so it can dry and catch as the fire grows.
whenToUse: When the ground is wet or snow-covered but you still need and are allowed to build a fire.
guides.preferredAction: Create a small platform of sticks or rocks, build your tinder and kindling on top, and use overhead cover only if it can be kept well away from flames.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot provide overhead cover, build a compact fire with a windward log wall and keep it small and controlled.
stepByStepActions: Lay a base of small sticks or rocks to raise the fire off wet ground.
Place tinder on the platform and arrange kindling in a teepee or log-cabin style.
Stack larger damp fuel nearby so it can pre-dry from the heat once the fire is established.
If using a tarp or poncho for rain protection, keep it high and far enough from heat to avoid melting or ignition.
Keep the fire small and compact for better heat and fuel efficiency.
warnings: Using overhead plastic or fabric covers near open flame is risky and must be constantly supervised.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in wet or windy conditions just because they are harder to start.
redFlags: Spreading flames into nearby vegetation or inability to control the fire due to wind or fuel conditions.
preparednessTips: Practice building small, efficient fires in safe settings before relying on them in emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Practical field fire-lay techniques
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common wet-ground and wet-weather fire-lay techniques taught in field courses.

guides.title: When Not to Attempt a Fire in Wet Conditions
guides.summary: Skip building a fire if it is unsafe, banned, or would distract from higher-priority tasks like shelter and getting dry.
guides.quickAnswer: Skip building a fire if it is unsafe, banned, or would distract from higher-priority tasks like shelter and getting dry.
whenToUse: Any wet, cold situation where building a fire is difficult or risky.
guides.preferredAction: Focus first on shelter, dry clothing, insulation, and food; treat fire as a backup tool only when conditions and safety allow.
guides.backupAction: Use chemical heat packs, movement if safe, or shared insulation if available.
stepByStepActions: Check local fire restrictions and evaluate wildfire risk.
Assess your skills, tools, and available dry tinder and fuel honestly.
If risk or difficulty is high, spend your time improving shelter, clothing, and bedding instead.
Maintain morale and body temperature with movement, warm drinks if possible, and social support.
warnings: Spending hours trying to start a fire in harsh conditions can leave you colder, wetter, and more exhausted.
whatNotToDo: Do not try risky improvisations with accelerants or unknown fuels out of frustration.
redFlags: You are wet, cold, unable to stop shivering, and lack safe fire or better shelter options.
preparednessTips: Plan so you rarely depend on fire alone: carry proper clothing, shelter, and emergency warming supplies.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia prevention principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing shelter, dryness, and insulation over marginal fire attempts.

guides.title: Fully Extinguishing Outdoor Fires
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.summary: Drown the fire with water, stir the ashes, and repeat until everything is cold to the touch; never leave warm or smoking material behind.
guides.quickAnswer: Drown the fire with water, stir the ashes, and repeat until everything is cold to the touch; never leave warm or smoking material behind.
whenToUse: Any time you are done with an outdoor fire or must leave it unattended.
guides.preferredAction: Use plenty of water and stirring to cool all coals, not just visible flames.
guides.backupAction: If water is scarce, use dirt or sand while mixing thoroughly, then check for remaining heat; avoid relying on soil alone in peat or root systems.
stepByStepActions: Let the fire burn down to ash and small coals if time allows.
Pour water over the entire fire area, including logs and surrounding ground.
Stir with a stick or tool, exposing hidden coals; add more water until hissing stops and all material is cool.
If using dirt or sand, mix thoroughly and check again for heat.
Feel carefully near the surface; if it is warm, repeat the cooling steps.
warnings: Poorly extinguished fires can smolder and later ignite wildfires, especially in dry or windy conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave coals or large unburned logs.
Do not bury a hot fire under thick soil without confirming it is cold.
redFlags: Smoke or flames reappearing after you thought the fire was out and beginning to spread beyond your control.
preparednessTips: Carry enough water for extinguishing or avoid fires altogether when water is limited and fire risk is high.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire-prevention fire-out guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative extinguishing practices consistent with park and wildfire-prevention messaging.

guides.title: Safe Use of Portable Heaters Indoors
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.summary: Keep space heaters three feet from anything that can burn, plug them directly into wall outlets, and turn them off when you sleep or leave the room.
guides.quickAnswer: Keep space heaters three feet from anything that can burn, plug them directly into wall outlets, and turn them off when you sleep or leave the room.
whenToUse: Using electric or fuel-fired portable heaters during cold weather or power shortages.
guides.preferredAction: Use modern, certified heaters with tip-over and overheat protection exactly as directed.
guides.backupAction: If you lack safe heaters, rely on clothing, blankets, and room-sealing techniques rather than unsafe heat sources.
stepByStepActions: Place the heater on a flat, stable, nonflammable surface.
Keep a three-foot kid- and pet-free zone around it.
Plug electric heaters directly into a wall outlet, never a power strip or extension cord.
Turn heaters off and unplug them when leaving the room or going to sleep.
Install and test smoke alarms and carbon monoxide detectors where appropriate.
warnings: Portable heaters are a leading cause of home heating fires and related deaths.
whatNotToDo: Do not use damaged heaters, run cords under rugs, use heaters near water, or use outdoor fuel heaters indoors.
redFlags: Burning smells, sparks, discolored outlets, or any small fire in the heater or nearby materials.
preparednessTips: Inspect heaters before cold seasons and keep alarms working with backup power.

guides.title: Fuel Storage and Handling During Emergencies
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guides.summary: Store fuels outside living areas in approved containers, away from heat sources, and never use them indoors for open burning.
guides.quickAnswer: Store fuels outside living areas in approved containers, away from heat sources, and never use them indoors for open burning.
whenToUse: Keeping gasoline, propane, or similar fuels on hand for generators, stoves, or vehicles during emergencies.
guides.preferredAction: Follow local rules for approved containers and store fuel outside the home, upright, sealed, and away from ignition sources.
guides.backupAction: If you must temporarily keep small quantities closer, ensure containers are closed, upright, and away from heat and sparks.
stepByStepActions: Use only approved, labeled fuel containers.
Store fuels outside living spaces in a shed or detached garage if possible.
Keep containers upright, sealed, and out of direct sunlight or high heat.
When refueling generators or stoves, turn them off and let them cool first.
Clean spills immediately and ventilate the area.
warnings: Improper storage raises risks of fire, explosion, and toxic fumes including carbon monoxide when fuels are burned.
whatNotToDo: Do not store fuel next to heaters, fireplaces, or cooking appliances.
Do not use gasoline or similar fuels to start indoor fires or stoves.
redFlags: Strong fuel odors inside the home, visible leaks, or any ignition of spilled fuel.
preparednessTips: Plan fuel needs realistically, avoid hoarding, and learn local storage and transport rules.

guides.title: Safe Options for Heating Indoors During Power Outages
guides.summary: Use built-in heating systems, properly vented fireplaces or wood stoves, and listed portable electric heaters; avoid unvented fuel devices.
guides.quickAnswer: Use built-in heating systems, properly vented fireplaces or wood stoves, and listed portable electric heaters; avoid unvented fuel devices.
whenToUse: Cold-weather power outages or heating failures when you must stay in place.
guides.preferredAction: Rely on safe, code-compliant heating systems plus room sealing and clothing layers.
guides.backupAction: Use portable electric heaters on generator power only if the generator is outside and all heater safety steps are followed.
stepByStepActions: Use central heating if it still works safely; otherwise move to a small room and focus on insulation and clothing.
If using a fireplace or wood stove, ensure the chimney or flue is clear and keep combustibles three feet away.
Use only modern listed electric space heaters indoors and follow heater safety rules.
If powering heaters by generator, keep the generator outdoors away from windows and vents and use proper cords.
Monitor carbon monoxide and smoke alarms and never block vents or flues.
warnings: Improper heating is a major cause of home fires and carbon monoxide poisoning during outages and cold spells.
whatNotToDo: Do not use charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens for space heating.
Do not bring generators, patio heaters, or gas heaters indoors.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion during heater use.
Any signs of fire or smoke from heating equipment.
preparednessTips: Have heating systems and chimneys inspected before winter and maintain working smoke and CO detectors.

guides.title: Unsafe Indoor Heating Methods to Avoid
guides.summary: Never use generators, charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens to heat your home; they can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning and fires.
guides.quickAnswer: Never use generators, charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens to heat your home; they can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning and fires.
whenToUse: Any cold-weather emergency where people might feel tempted to improvise heat indoors.
guides.preferredAction: Reject unsafe methods and instead rely on clothing, bedding, room sealing, and safe heaters or relocation to a warming center.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot stay warm without unsafe methods, move to a safer location if possible.
stepByStepActions: Identify clearly unsafe indoor heat sources such as generators, camping stoves, charcoal or gas grills, and gas ovens left open.
Commit to not using them inside homes, garages, or near windows and vents.
Use staying-warm-without-power methods instead.
If others suggest unsafe heating, show them clear CO and fire warnings.
warnings: CO is invisible and odorless; many people have died using generators and fuel-burning devices indoors for heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not bring generators inside or run them near windows.
Do not use stoves or ovens for house heating.
Do not sleep in rooms with burning charcoal or unvented fuel devices.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, or confusion when a fuel device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Install CO detectors with battery backup and learn which devices are safe only outdoors.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to Carbon Monoxide Exposure
guides.summary: Treat any CO alarm as an emergency: get everyone into fresh air immediately, then call emergency services from a safe location.
guides.quickAnswer: Treat any CO alarm as an emergency: get everyone into fresh air immediately, then call emergency services from a safe location.
whenToUse: Whenever a CO alarm activates or you suspect CO even without an alarm.
guides.preferredAction: Evacuate everyone outdoors, including pets, and call emergency services from outside.
guides.backupAction: If mobility issues or blocked exits make full evacuation difficult, move toward the nearest fresh air while waiting for help.
stepByStepActions: Do not ignore the alarm; stop what you are doing.
Get everyone out immediately using the quickest safe route.
Call emergency services and, if advised, your gas or utility company from outside.
Do not re-enter until authorities say it is safe.
Arrange inspection and repair of fuel-burning appliances and vents before using them again.
warnings: Opening windows instead of leaving may delay escape while CO levels remain dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not silence the alarm and continue normal activities.
Do not search for the source yourself while staying inside.
redFlags: Anyone collapses, has severe confusion, breathing trouble, or chest pain.
preparednessTips: Ensure everyone knows the CO alarm sound and escape routes.
Practice CO alarm drills the same way you practice fire drills.

guides.title: Deciding When Shelter Matters More Than Fire
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guides.summary: In most cold emergencies, getting under shelter and into dry, insulated clothing is more reliable and safer than trying to build a fire.
guides.quickAnswer: In most cold emergencies, getting under shelter and into dry, insulated clothing is more reliable and safer than trying to build a fire.
whenToUse: Whenever you are exposed to cold and considering spending time and energy making a fire.
guides.preferredAction: Prioritize wind and rain protection, dry clothing, and insulation from the ground first; treat fire as a supplement.
guides.backupAction: If no shelter or adequate clothing is possible, then consider a carefully chosen, safe fire as an additional heat source where allowed.
stepByStepActions: Assess wind, wetness, and available shelter options.
Secure shelter and dry layers first, even if simple.
Once reasonably protected, decide whether a fire adds enough benefit to justify the risk and effort.
If you choose to build a fire, follow fire-safety guidance closely.
warnings: Overemphasizing fire starting can waste critical time and energy while you continue losing heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay getting under shelter or changing out of wet clothes because you are focused on getting a fire going.
redFlags: Ongoing shivering, confusion, or clumsiness despite shelter and clothing improvements.
preparednessTips: Practice quick shelter setups and carry proper clothing so you do not depend on fire as a primary survival tool.

guides.title: Prioritizing Tasks in the First 30 Minutes of Exposure
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guides.summary: Use your first 30 minutes to get out of wind and rain, adjust clothing, protect hands and feet, and secure shelter; leave advanced fire tasks for later.
guides.quickAnswer: Use your first 30 minutes to get out of wind and rain, adjust clothing, protect hands and feet, and secure shelter; leave advanced fire tasks for later.
whenToUse: Onset of any cold exposure emergency such as getting lost, being stranded, or the start of a power outage.
guides.preferredAction: Follow a simple order: location safety, shelter, clothing/layers, insulation, then warmth/food and communication.
guides.backupAction: If you must move because of immediate danger, prioritize reaching safer terrain and minimum shelter before anything else.
stepByStepActions: Move away from immediate hazards such as rising water, unstable structures, or avalanche paths.
Get under any available shelter.
Adjust or change clothing to a drier layered setup; protect head, hands, and feet.
Insulate from the ground using whatever you have.
Only then think about fire, additional insulation, or signaling for help.
warnings: Poor early decisions such as staying in hazard areas or ignoring wet clothing quickly make later survival tasks harder.
whatNotToDo: Do not wander aimlessly in storms.
Do not spend your first half hour on low-priority tasks while still exposed.
redFlags: Immediate life threats such as rising water, structural collapse, or serious injury take precedence over warmth decisions.
preparednessTips: Mentally rehearse a simple first-30-minutes checklist for your climate and activities and share it with family or group members.

Turn off or unplug major appliances and electronics to reduce surge damage when power returns.

Use flashlights or battery lanterns for light and keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed.
Conserve phone battery and monitor official updates by radio, SMS, or trusted apps.

guides.title: Keeping Food Safe During a Power Outage
guides.parentTopic: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages
guides.summary: Keep fridge and freezer doors closed; a refrigerator keeps food safe about 4 hours, and a full freezer 24–48 hours, if doors stay shut.
guides.quickAnswer: Keep fridge and freezer doors closed; a refrigerator keeps food safe about 4 hours, and a full freezer 24–48 hours, if doors stay shut.
whenToUse: As soon as power goes out and through the first day of an outage.
guides.preferredAction: Keep refrigerator doors shut, minimize freezer opening, and move perishables to coolers with ice only if the outage will exceed safe timelines.
guides.backupAction: If you lack ice or coolers, keep doors closed and plan to discard perishable refrigerated food after 4 hours without power.
stepByStepActions: Check that fridge and freezer temperatures are at safe ranges using appliance thermometers if you have them.
Immediately stop opening the refrigerator; use a cooler for any needed items if available.
Remember the main timelines: about 4 hours for an unopened fridge, about 48 hours for a full unopened freezer, and about 24 hours for a half-full freezer.
If the outage will likely last longer than 4 hours, move critical perishables to a cooler with ice if you can keep them at or below 4 C / 40 F.
warnings: Food in the danger zone above 4 C / 40 F for more than 2 hours can cause foodborne illness.
Taste and smell are not reliable indicators of safety.
whatNotToDo: Do not sample questionable food to see if it is safe.
Do not refreeze food that has been above 4 C / 40 F for more than 2 hours.
Do not rely on cool outside air unless you can keep food at safe, monitored temperatures.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, or severe abdominal pain after eating food stored during an outage.
Dehydration symptoms in vulnerable household members.
preparednessTips: Keep appliance thermometers in fridge and freezer.
Store ice packs and frozen water bottles to buy extra time during outages.
Maintain a supply of shelf-stable foods that do not require refrigeration.

Protect it from rain or snow only with a cover designed for safe ventilation.

CO alarm sounds.
Sparks, smoke, or unusual generator noises.
preparednessTips: Decide in advance where you will place a generator safely.

Store fuel safely in approved containers away from living spaces and heat sources.

guides.title: Carbon Monoxide Basics and Symptoms
guides.summary: Carbon monoxide is an odorless, colorless gas from fuel-burning devices that can kill quickly; early symptoms include headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, and confusion.
guides.quickAnswer: Carbon monoxide is an odorless, colorless gas from fuel-burning devices that can kill quickly; early symptoms include headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, and confusion.
whenToUse: To understand CO risk whenever generators, heaters, stoves, grills, or vehicles may be used near buildings.
guides.preferredAction: Prevent exposure by keeping fuel-burning devices outside or properly vented and by installing working CO detectors.
guides.backupAction: If you suspect CO exposure, get everyone into fresh air immediately and call emergency services.
stepByStepActions: Learn the common CO sources in homes and outage situations.
Recognize early symptoms such as headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, and confusion.
Treat a cluster of these symptoms around fuel appliances as a possible CO emergency.
Remember that sleeping or intoxicated people may not notice symptoms before they collapse.
warnings: CO cannot be seen, smelled, or tasted.
CO poisoning can be fatal within minutes at high levels.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore mild symptoms shared by multiple people in a home with fuel devices running.
Do not assume opening a window is adequate protection when a device is being used unsafely.
redFlags: CO alarm sounds.
Multiple people have sudden flu-like symptoms without fever.
Unconsciousness, severe confusion, or chest pain near fuel appliances.
preparednessTips: Install battery-powered or battery-backup CO detectors on every level and near sleeping areas.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries at least yearly.
Have fuel-burning appliances inspected and serviced regularly.

whenToUse: When planning or beginning treks, climbs, or travel above roughly 2,500 to 3,000 meters, especially if you are not acclimatized.
guides.preferredAction: Limit sleeping-altitude gain, schedule acclimatization days, and adjust plans based on symptoms.
guides.backupAction: If you must ascend faster, reduce exertion, monitor symptoms closely, and be ready to stop or descend early.
stepByStepActions: Above roughly 3,000 meters, plan no more than about 300 to 500 meters increase in sleeping altitude per day and include rest days every few days.
Maintain steady hydration and eat regular nutritious meals.
Avoid alcohol and sedatives, especially at night.
Watch closely for headache, nausea, unusual fatigue, sleep disruption, or worsening symptoms and stop ascending if they intensify.
warnings: Rapid ascent increases the risk of altitude illness, which can become life-threatening if ignored.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore persistent or worsening symptoms while ascending.
Do not continue to higher sleeping altitude if you already have clear signs of altitude sickness.
redFlags: Severe headache, repeated vomiting, trouble walking straight, shortness of breath at rest, or altered behavior at altitude.
preparednessTips: Use region-specific altitude profiles and conservative ascent plans for routes such as the Himalaya.
Encourage pre-trip consultation with experienced providers about medications and higher-risk conditions.

guides.summary: Confusion, chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, seizures, or loss of consciousness during weather exposure are medical emergencies.
guides.quickAnswer: Confusion, chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, seizures, or loss of consciousness during weather exposure are medical emergencies.
whenToUse: Any time someone becomes very unwell during extreme heat or cold, smoke or dust exposure, storms, or after a lightning strike.
guides.preferredAction: Recognize these signs quickly, move to the relevant emergency action guide, and call for help immediately.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot call right away, begin first aid and get help from others nearby as fast as possible.
stepByStepActions: Check for red-flag symptoms such as confusion, inability to walk or talk normally, chest pain, severe breathing difficulty, seizures, or unresponsiveness.
In heat, suspect heat stroke if the person is very hot, confused, or collapsed and call emergency services while cooling.
In cold, suspect severe hypothermia if the person is very cold, confused, or not shivering and call for help while warming gently.

After lightning, treat all strikes as emergencies even if the person initially seems okay.

whatNotToDo: Do not delay calling for help to see whether severe symptoms go away.
Do not rely only on over-the-counter medications when severe symptoms are present.
redFlags: Confusion, collapse, chest pain, severe trouble breathing, seizures, or unresponsiveness after weather exposure.

guides.title: STOP-Think-Observe-Plan (First 30 Minutes)
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guides.summary: Pause, calm down, check for injuries and hazards, then make a simple plan instead of walking blindly.
guides.quickAnswer: Pause, calm down, check for injuries and hazards, then make a simple plan instead of walking blindly.
whenToUse: Any time you realize you are lost, stranded, off-route, or unexpectedly stuck outdoors.
guides.preferredAction: Use the STOP method, then act on the safest, simplest short plan.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot think clearly, focus only on immediate safety: move out of danger, treat obvious bleeding, get out of wind or rain, then rest and reassess.
stepByStepActions: Stop in a safe spot away from immediate hazards such as rockfall, traffic, avalanche terrain, surf, or fire.
Breathe slowly for 30 to 60 seconds to reduce panic.
Check yourself and others quickly for serious bleeding, breathing problems, or inability to move.
Observe the time of day, weather, terrain, nearby landmarks, last known point, and any cell signal or GPS information.
Ask what the main threat is in the next few hours: cold, heat, darkness, dehydration, or injury.
Make a simple 15 to 30 minute plan such as staying put, improving shelter, and signaling for help.
If possible, text or call emergency services and a trusted contact with your situation and last known location.
Carry out the short plan, then pause and reassess.
warnings: Panic and aimless wandering are major causes of worsening lost-person outcomes.
Poor decisions made in the first 30 minutes can be hard to reverse in bad terrain or bad weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to do something if you are unsure where you are.
Do not ignore injuries or severe cold or heat to push on a bit more.
Do not rely only on your memory of the route if you are clearly disoriented.
redFlags: Severe bleeding, difficulty breathing, altered mental status, or inability to walk.
Rapidly worsening weather with poor shelter or warm or dry clothing.
Nightfall close at hand with no light, no clear route, or no shelter.
Immediate terrain hazards such as flooding, avalanche slopes, cliffs, or surf.
preparednessTips: Share a trip plan with a trusted person including route and return time.
Carry a whistle, map, compass, charged phone, power bank, and simple emergency kit.
Practice the STOP method on day hikes so it feels automatic under stress.

guides.title: Simple Survival Priorities: What Comes First

guides.summary: For most short-term emergencies, focus on safety, first aid, shelter or warmth, signaling, and then water; food and complex tasks can wait.
guides.quickAnswer: For most short-term emergencies, focus on safety, first aid, shelter or warmth, signaling, and then water; food and complex tasks can wait.
whenToUse: When making a quick plan for the next hours after becoming stranded or after an emergency.
guides.preferredAction: Follow a simple order: safety and severe injuries, shelter and temperature, signaling and communication, water, then food and extras.
guides.backupAction: If conditions are mild, adjust the order slightly but still avoid spending early energy on low-value tasks like foraging or elaborate construction.
stepByStepActions: Check airway, breathing, severe bleeding, and immediate hazards first.
Get out of wind, rain, or direct sun and adjust clothing to avoid hypothermia or heat illness.
Use phone, whistle, mirror, bright clothing, or ground signals early to make yourself findable.
Identify the safest available water source and plan how to treat it.
Conserve energy rather than hunting, foraging, or building complex structures in most short-term events.
warnings: Hypothermia or heat illness can kill in hours, long before hunger.
Treating water is important, but usually less urgent than staying warm or cool.
whatNotToDo: Do not spend your first hour on complex shelters or food gathering if you lack basic shelter or signaling.
Do not ignore obvious hazards while focused on secondary tasks.
Do not assume you must find food immediately.
redFlags: Signs of hypothermia or severe heat illness.
Ongoing severe bleeding, chest pain, difficulty breathing, or altered consciousness.
preparednessTips: Learn basic first aid and CPR through Red Cross or similar training.
Pack an emergency kit that supports shelter, signaling, and water treatment first.
Practice short priority drills on day hikes.

guides.backupAction: If you cannot make fire or large symbols, hang bright clothing or reflective materials in visible places and keep your area obvious.

Use rocks, logs, or gear to make letters or shapes such as SOS, X, or a large arrow at least several feet wide.
If fire is safe and allowed, build small signal fires and be ready to add green material for smoke when searchers are near.
Drape bright clothing or a reflective blanket over rocks or trees to create strong contrast.
At night, group three lights or fires in a line or triangle to signal distress.
Maintain signals and check that they remain visible.

Smoke can worsen breathing or hide your position if poorly placed.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in high wind, extreme dryness, or near flammable brush.
Do not create cluttered ground markings; simple, large shapes work best.
redFlags: If you see aircraft or hear searchers, focus on boosting visibility quickly.
If fire escapes your control or wind shifts dangerously, move away immediately and reassess.
preparednessTips: Carry a reflective emergency blanket and high-visibility clothing.

guides.title: Choosing the Safest Available Water Source
guides.summary: Pick the clearest, most upstream, and least contaminated-looking source you can, then plan to treat it.
guides.quickAnswer: Pick the clearest, most upstream, and least contaminated-looking source you can, then plan to treat it.
whenToUse: Whenever you must collect natural water for drinking or hygiene in the field.
guides.preferredAction: Select flowing water from higher, cleaner-looking locations and avoid sources near livestock, sewage, or heavy human activity.
guides.backupAction: If only poor-quality sources are available, choose the least bad option and treat it as thoroughly as your tools allow.
stepByStepActions: Look for flowing water rather than stagnant pools and avoid water with strong odor, scum, or dead animals.
Collect from upstream of camps, trails, or livestock areas whenever possible.
Prefer water that is clear to the eye; if silty, allow sediment to settle before treatment.
Avoid collecting from beaver ponds, irrigation channels, or near latrines and wastewater discharge.
Treat the water before drinking whenever possible.
warnings: Even clear high-mountain water can contain microorganisms.
Contamination risk is higher downstream of agriculture, grazing, or human settlement.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume natural equals safe.
Do not drink from obviously contaminated sources unless you face life-threatening dehydration and have no alternative.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, or dehydration after drinking untreated water.
Known compromise of a public or shared supply after a disaster.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one reliable treatment method on extended trips.
Learn local guidance about water safety in your destination.
Bring enough stored water for the first day where feasible.

Fill containers directly or pour from a cleaner cup or pot rather than dipping with dirty hands.
Keep untreated water separate from treated water.
Cover containers tightly to keep out dirt and insects.
Store in shade if possible and away from fuel, chemicals, or latrine areas.

whenToUse: In extreme situations where you cannot treat water and are showing signs of severe dehydration.
guides.preferredAction: Choose the cleanest-appearing source available and drink enough to stabilize, understanding the infection risk.
guides.backupAction: If you can delay briefly, rest, reduce exertion, and see whether you can improvise treatment before deciding.
stepByStepActions: Assess severe dehydration signs such as dry mouth, minimal urine, dizziness, confusion, or inability to keep walking.
Confirm you truly cannot treat water with fire, fuel, filter, tablets, or safe containers.
Select the least contaminated source available.
Drink slowly at first, especially if nauseated.
After rescue or reaching care, tell medical personnel that you drank untreated water.

Wash utensils and dishes with safe water and soap if possible, then air-dry.
Store food away from waste and animals.

Do not use clearly contaminated water on food-contact items if you can avoid it.

Signs of dehydration such as dizziness, confusion, or very low urine output.
preparednessTips: Pack travel-sized soap, alcohol-based sanitizer, and resealable waste bags.

guides.title: Simple Loops and Attachment Knots (Figure-Eight on a Bight, Bowline)
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guides.summary: Use simple, non-slipping loops to attach lines to objects or create fixed loops for gear; do not use them for life-safety loads unless trained.
guides.quickAnswer: Use simple, non-slipping loops to attach lines to objects or create fixed loops for gear; do not use them for life-safety loads unless trained.

Avoid cutting in the area between your thighs where a slip can hit major blood vessels.
Cut away from your body and hands using stable support where possible.

Stop and reassess if you feel rushed, overloaded, or unstable.

Do not cut toward your own body or hold material in your lap while cutting.

whenToUse: When deciding whether a task truly justifies pulling out your knife.
guides.preferredAction: Ask whether you can solve the problem another way without adding blade risk.
guides.backupAction: If you must use a knife while not at your best, slow down, reduce the task scope, and consider whether a steadier person can do it instead.
stepByStepActions: Check whether your hands are cold or numb, whether you are very tired, or whether you are emotionally stressed.
Consider alternatives such as moving the shelter, using a different anchor, or breaking branches by hand.
If you proceed, choose the smallest and simplest cut that will work.
Stop after short intervals to reassess your control and need.
warnings: Many serious outdoor knife injuries happen at the end of the day when people are cold and tired.
Panic-driven or tactical uses of knives increase risk without improving safety.
whatNotToDo: Do not use knives for wildlife self-defense plans; follow official wildlife safety guidance instead.
Do not attempt knife skills you have only seen online without prior safe practice.
redFlags: Any deep or heavily bleeding knife wound.
Wounds with loss of movement or feeling in fingers or toes.
preparednessTips: Reduce knife dependence with pre-cut cord lengths or prepared stakes.
Train on safe tool use before extended trips.

guides.title: Pocket Always-With-You Kit
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guides.summary: Carry a tiny set of essentials—whistle, light, basic bandages, fire starter, and emergency blanket—on your person, not just in your pack.
guides.quickAnswer: Carry a tiny set of essentials—whistle, light, basic bandages, fire starter, and emergency blanket—on your person, not just in your pack.
whenToUse: Everyday and on any outdoor trip where you might be separated from your main pack.
guides.preferredAction: Assemble a small kit that fits in a pocket or worn pouch and covers signaling, light, basic first aid, and minimal shelter.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot carry a full micro-kit, prioritize a whistle, small flashlight, and a way to contact help.
stepByStepActions: Include a whistle, small flashlight or headlamp, adhesive bandages, alcohol wipes, gauze, and tape.
Add a compact emergency blanket, lighter or matches if allowed, and a few water treatment tablets where appropriate.
Keep the kit on your body, not only in your backpack.
Check items regularly and replace dead batteries and expired supplies.
warnings: Relying only on a larger kit in your pack can leave you vulnerable if you are separated from it.
Some items such as lighters may be restricted in specific environments.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload the pocket kit so much that you stop carrying it.
Do not assume your phone alone is enough; batteries and signal can fail.
redFlags: If you use critical items such as all your bandages or water tablets, treat that as a cue to shorten the trip or seek safer conditions.
preparednessTips: Adapt official emergency kit guidance to pocket scale.
Customize for local conditions such as extra warmth in cold climates or sun protection in hot climates.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official checklist that supports small, practical grab-and-go essentials such as light, whistle, and first aid.

guides.title: Daypack-Size Emergency Kit for Hikes
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guides.summary: Add core essentials to your hiking pack: navigation, light, extra clothing, shelter, water, food, first aid, fire, tools, and signaling.
guides.quickAnswer: Add core essentials to your hiking pack: navigation, light, extra clothing, shelter, water, food, first aid, fire, tools, and signaling.
whenToUse: For day hikes, short trips, or local outings where an unplanned night out is possible.
guides.preferredAction: Pack items that let you survive an unplanned night outdoors: extra warmth, rain protection, emergency shelter, water and treatment, first aid, light, and signaling.
guides.backupAction: If space or budget is limited, prioritize shelter or warmth, light, signaling, and water over duplicate tools.
stepByStepActions: Carry map, compass, and or offline GPS or phone maps.
Pack an extra warm layer, hat, light rain shell, and emergency blanket or bivy.
Bring a headlamp and spare batteries.
Carry enough water for the day plus a treatment method.
Add extra high-energy snacks.
Pack a basic first aid kit, blister care, whistle, reflective item, and a small knife or multi-tool used conservatively.
warnings: Overpacking can lead to leaving the kit behind; find a balance you will actually carry.
Some locations restrict open flame and certain tools.
whatNotToDo: Do not pack only cool gear while neglecting basics like warm clothing or water treatment.
Do not assume group members will share kits; everyone should have basics.
redFlags: If your kit cannot cover an unplanned night in current weather, consider shortening or turning back from the trip.
preparednessTips: Use official emergency kit checklists as a baseline and adapt for hiking.
Review your kit after each trip and adjust based on what you used or missed.

guides.title: Signs You Must Switch to Shelter and Warmth
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.summary: If you are getting cold, wet, or exhausted, stop non-essential tasks and put your energy into staying warm, dry, and out of the wind.
guides.quickAnswer: If you are getting cold, wet, or exhausted, stop non-essential tasks and put your energy into staying warm, dry, and out of the wind.
whenToUse: When weather worsens, daylight fades, or you notice early signs of hypothermia or heat stress.
guides.preferredAction: Pause other activities and work on simple, effective shelter and clothing adjustments.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot build shelter, move to more protected terrain, huddle with others, and use any available insulation and emergency blankets.
stepByStepActions: Watch for uncontrollable shivering, clumsiness, confusion, or slurred speech in cold conditions.
If present, stop tasks like exploring or building elaborate shelters.
Add dry layers, get out of wind or rain, insulate from the ground, and share body warmth if appropriate.
In hot conditions, move to shade, loosen clothing, sip safe water, and rest.
Once basic comfort is restored, re-evaluate priorities and continue only essential tasks.
warnings: Hypothermia and heat stroke can progress quickly and quietly.
Continuing physical work in these conditions can push you into collapse.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to stay warm if you are wet, exhausted, and unsure of direction.
Do not ignore shivering or confusion in yourself or others.
redFlags: Confusion, loss of coordination, or collapse in cold or hot environments.
Shivering that stops suddenly while the person is still cold.
preparednessTips: Pack adequate clothing and an emergency blanket even on day trips.
Learn signs of hypothermia and heat illness through formal first-aid training.
Check forecasts and plan for worse-than-expected conditions.

guides.title: When to Drop Tasks and Focus on Signaling
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.summary: When rescuers are likely searching for you or you see or hear them, stop extra tasks and focus on being seen and heard.
guides.quickAnswer: When rescuers are likely searching for you or you see or hear them, stop extra tasks and focus on being seen and heard.
whenToUse: Any time you suspect a search has started or see aircraft, drones, boats, or search teams.
guides.preferredAction: Shift energy to whistle use, mirror or light flashes, and making your location obvious with ground or visual signals.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot actively signal continuously, set up passive signals like bright clothing or reflective blankets where they will be most visible.
stepByStepActions: When you hear or see potential rescuers, stop non-essential tasks.
Move to a visible area if safe, bringing whistle, mirror, and bright items.
Use three-blast whistle signals and mirror or light flashes toward rescuers.
Stand or place bright gear where you contrast with the surroundings.
Once contact seems likely, stay in place unless directed to move.
warnings: Missing a narrow window of visibility can delay rescue significantly.
Over-focusing on camp tasks can make you miss distant sounds or signs of search teams.
whatNotToDo: Do not continue long, noisy tasks that mask searcher calls or aircraft noise.
Do not hide under dense canopy when you could safely stand in a clearing.
redFlags: If your condition is deteriorating, communicate that clearly as soon as contact is made.
preparednessTips: Keep signaling tools accessible, not buried in your pack.
Establish a group plan so everyone pauses and signals when search activity appears.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative field guide stressing signaling early and using whistles, mirrors, and beacons.

guides.title: Balancing Activity and Rest to Avoid Exhaustion
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.summary: Pace yourself; overexertion increases risk of injury, poor decisions, and hypothermia or heat illness.
guides.quickAnswer: Pace yourself; overexertion increases risk of injury, poor decisions, and hypothermia or heat illness.
whenToUse: Throughout any extended emergency or survival situation.
guides.preferredAction: Alternate periods of necessary work with rest, and stop tasks before you become exhausted or confused.
guides.backupAction: If you are already exhausted, simplify your plan and focus only on shelter, warmth, and minimal signaling until you recover.
stepByStepActions: After initial planning, schedule short work blocks followed by rest, especially in difficult conditions.
Keep tasks simple and high-value; avoid complex projects when tired.
Monitor yourself and others for fatigue, irritability, and poor coordination.
Adjust plans as daylight changes and avoid demanding work in the dark unless absolutely necessary.
warnings: Exhaustion and low blood sugar impair judgment.
In cold or wet conditions, activity can hide early hypothermia until you stop moving.
whatNotToDo: Do not insist on finishing non-critical tasks when you are clearly beyond your limits.
Do not work in hazardous terrain when extremely tired or in the dark unless it is the lesser danger.
redFlags: Severe confusion, inability to follow simple instructions, or repeated falls.
Chest pain or shortness of breath not explained by mild exertion.
preparednessTips: Maintain basic fitness and health.
Bring enough food and water to reduce exhaustion and dehydration risk.
